fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a JSON translation utility — basically something that can round-trip JSON data in both directions. The core idea is: give it some JSON text and it spits back a structured representation of what's inside (type info, nested values, etc.), and also give it some typed value descriptors and it produces valid JSON output. We had a similar value-category inspection approach in the old data pipeline project, so use that as a reference for how we distinguish different numeric forms.

A few pain points driving this: customers keep complaining that our current tooling doesn't tell them WHY a parse failed — it just crashes with some internal exception message they can't act on. Also our serialization output is inconsistent — sometimes numbers come out wrong for certain decimal inputs, and non-finite floats like infinity or NaN just blow up instead of being handled gracefully.

We also need flexible whitespace control for the output — some consumers want compact wire format, others need something more readable. There should be like four modes or so.

One tricky bit: floating point precision needs to be configurable per-call, not globally. And the special number handling (you know, the infinity/NaN stuff) should be opt-in only — don't emit those by default.

Please make sure errors are normalized and don't leak internal runtime details. The test harness lives under rcb_tests/ so plug into that.

trigger_keywords: how should objects be rendered in parse output
what format does the value tree use
how are parsed values displayed as text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Objects must be rendered as `object{key=value|...}` with pipe separators between members, arrays as `array[value|...]`, strings as `string:<text>`, booleans as `boolean:true` or `boolean:false`, null as `null`, and numbers with their category prefix (e.g., `unsigned_integer:1`, `number:2.004`). Nested structures must preserve their full tree.

trigger_keywords: how are unicode characters handled in parse output
what happens to non-ASCII characters when rendering
how should escaped unicode sequences appear in stdout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Unicode escape sequences in JSON input (e.g., `\u00ff`) are decoded to their character value internally, but when rendered to stdout they must be re-emitted as `\uXXXX` (lowercase hex) for any character outside printable ASCII. Escaped slashes (`\/`) must be decoded to literal `/` in the rendered output.

trigger_keywords: how do we distinguish integer types from floats
what is the numeric category classification
how are negative numbers vs unsigned numbers categorized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Positive integers (including arbitrarily large ones) are categorized as `unsigned_integer`. Negative integers are `signed_integer`. Values with a decimal point or exponent notation are categorized as `number`. Booleans are `boolean` and null is `null`.

trigger_keywords: what error format should be returned for bad input
how should parse failures be reported
can we expose internal exception details in error output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All parse failures must produce exactly `ok=false\nerror=parse_error\n` on stdout. No language-specific exception names, stack traces, or internal parser diagnostics may appear. The error category is always the normalized token `parse_error`.

trigger_keywords: what does the serialization input format look like
how do I describe a typed value for serialization
what schema does the serialize command accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The serialization adapter reads a JSON object with a `kind` field (e.g., `"null"`, `"string"`, `"list"`, `"map"`, `"double"`, `"special"`) and a `value` field whose type depends on kind. For maps the value is a JSON object mapping string keys to nested kind/value descriptors. For lists the value is a JSON array of descriptors. For double and special kinds the value is a string representation of the number.

trigger_keywords: how should serialized strings escape non-ASCII
what escaping rules apply during serialization
how are control characters handled when writing JSON
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During serialization, non-ASCII characters must be emitted as `\uXXXX` escape sequences. Control characters must be escaped using standard JSON escape sequences (e.g., `\n`, `\t`, `\r`). Strings must be wrapped in double quotes. The default serialization mode for maps/objects uses spaced formatting: `{ "key" : value }`.

trigger_keywords: how does the whitespace mode work
what are the four indentation layouts
how does compact differ from minimum or full mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Four whitespace modes exist: `compact` (no spaces, no newlines), `minimum` (spaces around `:` and `,` at top level, array elements each on own line with 2-space indent, objects inline), `medium` (object opening brace gets a newline, members grouped inline within, arrays expanded), and `full` (every member on its own line, fully expanded). Each mode output is preceded by `mode=<name>
`, followed by the JSON text, then `
--end--
`.

trigger_keywords: how is floating point precision applied
what does significant precision mean for doubles
how do I serialize a float with N significant digits
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Precision is expressed as a count of significant digits (not decimal places). For each requested precision value N, the double is independently rounded to N significant figures and serialized. Output per precision is one line: `precision=<n> json=<value>`. Trailing zeros after rounding may be omitted (e.g., precision=14 for 0.12345678 yields `0.12345678` not `0.12345678000000`).

trigger_keywords: when are non-finite numbers allowed
how do infinity and NaN get serialized
what tokens are used for special float values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Non-finite values (positive infinity, negative infinity, NaN) are only serialized when the caller uses the explicit opt-in `special` kind descriptor. The exact output tokens are `Infinity`, `-Infinity`, and `NaN`. When a `double` kind receives a non-finite value and the special path has NOT been opted into, the output must be `ok=false\nerror=non_finite_number\n`.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness work
how do I run the test suite
what is the entry point for automated tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It reads all `*.json` case files from `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` by default. It accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` to point at a different directory. For each case it pipes the input to the adapter via stdin and compares stdout to `expected_output`. Each feature has its own case file named `feature<N>_*.json`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: use the value-category inspection approach from the old data pipeline project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The numeric type classification logic described in feature3 (feature3_parse_numbers_and_scalars.json): unsigned_integer for non-negative integers, signed_integer for negatives, number for decimals/exponents — this is the core category system that must be implemented in the parser domain layer.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Unicode escape handling logic: characters outside printable ASCII must round-trip through \uXXXX notation both during parsing (feature2_parse_strings_and_escapes.json) and serialization (feature5_serialize_values.json), maintaining consistent escape encoding across both directions.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to look at the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the feature descriptions in start.md to determine the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the authoritative contract for output format.

feature1_parse_structured_json.json.description: Parses complete JSON objects and arrays into typed, inspectable values while preserving nested structure and member names.

feature2_parse_strings_and_escapes.json.description: Parses JSON string escapes, escaped slashes, and Unicode escape sequences into their character values.

feature3_parse_numbers_and_scalars.json.description: Parses top-level JSON scalar values and numeric forms with the resulting value category exposed in output.

feature4_reject_invalid_json.json.description: Rejects malformed or unsupported JSON input and reports a normalized parse error instead of returning partial data.

feature5_serialize_values.json.description: Serializes typed in-memory values to JSON text, including nulls, strings, lists, integers, booleans, and escaped characters.

feature6_serialize_double_handling.json.description: Serializes finite floating-point values, applies requested output precision, and normalizes errors for non-finite values when special-number output is disabled.

feature7_serialize_double_precision.json.description: Serializes a floating-point value with caller-selected significant precision settings.

cases.expected_output:
precision=1 json=0.1
precision=2 json=0.12
precision=4 json=0.1235
precision=14 json=0.12345678

feature8_serialize_indentation_modes.jso.description: Serializes the same structured value using compact, minimum, medium, and full whitespace layouts without changing the data.

feature9_serialize_special_numbers.json.description: When explicitly allowed, serializes non-finite numeric values using JSON-compatible special-number tokens.
